summary: Patrick McKenzie and Keith Perhac discuss their experiences working in tech in Japan, covering the stark differences in how programmers are valued compared to the US, the broken social contract of lifetime employment, and Japan's lagging adoption of modern web practices. They pivot to evangelizing A/B testing as a transformative business practice, sharing concrete examples of massive conversion lifts from simple changes like headlines and button design. The core argument is that A/B testing is criminally underused despite being nearly free to implement, and that the skill of conversion optimization is far more valuable than most engineering work. They also discuss how job titles matter enormously — calling yourself a 'system engineer' instead of 'programmer' can double your perceived value.
key_points: Japanese tech culture treats programmers as code monkeys while 'system engineers' command double the pay and respect — a dynamic that applies everywhere, not just Japan
A/B testing is so cheap to implement (one line of code, five minutes) that NOT doing it is almost always a waste relative to other business activities
A random suggestion from someone who knew nothing about Bingo Card Creator produced a 40% lift in sales just by changing a headline
Most businesses don't track basic metrics that are key to their success, like what percentage of users return after first use (typically a depressing ~40%)
Press credibility ('As seen in the New York Times') and faces on landing pages reliably boost conversions — people are biologically wired to track gazes
money_quotes: If there are 10 people in a fight and only one of them understands how to use science, I already know who's going to win.
People do not read on the Internet. If you take one thing from this podcast, people do not read on this Internet.
I guarantee if you run a business right now, there is some number that is key to your business that you are not tracking and that would be very eye-opening to you.
On the medical science scale, the discipline of design is closer to leeches than it is to medicine.
The feeling in the company was that even though we were a software company, writing software did not add value. That's almost a direct quote from the CEO.

If you can routinely quote numbers like that, you are no longer in the $60-to-$100-an-hour bucket with other programmers, you are in the bucket with strategic initiatives that add five percent to Bank of America's bottom line.

Think back. A week ago you were doing something for your business, right? Did it get you 40 percent to 60 percent improvement in your business's bottom line? If not, why weren't you doing A/B testing instead?
Whatever day God handed out the taste I was studying stats for D&D characters or something and totally missed it.
key_insight: A/B testing is so cheap and so powerful that failing to do it continuously is one of the largest unforced errors a business can make, yet most companies — even sophisticated ones — leave enormous gains on the table.
